speaker: SAMPSON
text: Gregory, on my word we’ll not carry coals.

speaker: GREGORY
text: No, for then we should be colliers.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: I mean, an we be in choler, we’ll draw.

speaker: GREGORY
text: Ay, while you live, draw your neck out of

speaker: GREGORY
text: collar.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: I strike quickly, being moved.

speaker: GREGORY
text: But thou art not quickly moved to strike.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: A dog of the house of Montague moves me.

speaker: GREGORY
text: To move is to stir, and to be valiant is to

speaker: GREGORY
text: stand. Therefore if thou art moved thou runn’st

speaker: GREGORY
text: away.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: A dog of that house shall move me to stand. I

speaker: SAMPSON
text: will take the wall of any man or maid of Montague’s.

speaker: GREGORY
text: That shows thee a weak slave, for the weakest

speaker: GREGORY
text: goes to the wall.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: ’Tis true, and therefore women, being the

speaker: SAMPSON
text: weaker vessels, are ever thrust to the wall. Therefore

speaker: SAMPSON
text: I will push Montague’s men from the wall and

speaker: SAMPSON
text: thrust his maids to the wall.

speaker: GREGORY
text: The quarrel is between our masters and us

speaker: GREGORY
text: their men.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: ’Tis all one. I will show myself a tyrant.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: When I have fought with the men, I will be civil

speaker: SAMPSON
text: with the maids; I will cut off their heads.

speaker: GREGORY
text: The heads of the maids?

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Ay, the heads of the maids, or their maidenheads.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Take it in what sense thou wilt.

speaker: GREGORY
text: They must take it in sense that feel it.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Me they shall feel while I am able to stand,

speaker: SAMPSON
text: and ’tis known I am a pretty piece of flesh.

speaker: GREGORY
text: ’Tis well thou art not fish; if thou hadst, thou

speaker: GREGORY
text: hadst been poor-john. Draw thy tool. Here comes

speaker: GREGORY
text: of the house of Montagues.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: My naked weapon is out. Quarrel, I will back

speaker: SAMPSON
text: thee.

speaker: GREGORY
text: How? Turn thy back and run?

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Fear me not.

speaker: GREGORY
text: No, marry. I fear thee!

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Let us take the law of our sides; let them

speaker: SAMPSON
text: begin.

speaker: GREGORY
text: I will frown as I pass by, and let them take it

speaker: GREGORY
text: as they list.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Nay, as they dare. I will bite my thumb at

speaker: SAMPSON
text: them, which is disgrace to them if they bear it.

speaker: ABRAM
text: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

speaker: SAMPSON
text: I do bite my thumb, sir.

speaker: ABRAM
text: Do you bite your thumb at us, sir?

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Is the law of our side if I

speaker: SAMPSON
text: say “Ay”?

speaker: GREGORY
text: No.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: No, sir, I do not bite my thumb at you, sir,

speaker: SAMPSON
text: but I bite my thumb, sir.

speaker: GREGORY
text: Do you quarrel, sir?

speaker: ABRAM
text: Quarrel, sir? No, sir.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: But if you do, sir, I am for you. I serve as

speaker: SAMPSON
text: good a man as you.

speaker: ABRAM
text: No better.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Well, sir.

speaker: GREGORY
text: Say “better”; here comes

speaker: GREGORY
text: one of my master’s kinsmen.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Yes, better, sir.

speaker: ABRAM
text: You lie.

speaker: SAMPSON
text: Draw if you be men.—Gregory, remember

speaker: SAMPSON
text: thy washing blow.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Part, fools!

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Put up your swords. You know not what you do.

speaker: TYBALT
text: What, art thou drawn among these heartless hinds?

speaker: TYBALT
text: Turn thee, Benvolio; look upon thy death.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: I do but keep the peace. Put up thy sword,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Or manage it to part these men with me.

speaker: TYBALT
text: What, drawn and talk of peace? I hate the word

speaker: TYBALT
text: As I hate hell, all Montagues, and thee.

speaker: TYBALT
text: Have at thee, coward!

speaker: CITIZENS
text: Clubs, bills, and partisans! Strike! Beat them down!

speaker: CITIZENS
text: Down with the Capulets! Down with the Montagues!

speaker: CAPULET
text: What noise is this? Give me my long sword, ho!

speaker:
LADY
               
              CAPULET
text: A crutch, a crutch! Why call you for a sword?

speaker: CAPULET
text: My sword, I say. Old Montague is come

speaker: CAPULET
text: And flourishes his blade in spite of me.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Thou villain Capulet!—Hold me not; let me go.

speaker:
LADY
               
              MONTAGUE
text: Thou shalt not stir one foot to seek a foe.

speaker: PRINCE
text: Rebellious subjects, enemies to peace,

speaker: PRINCE
text: Profaners of this neighbor-stainèd steel—

speaker: PRINCE
text: Will they not hear?—What ho! You men, you beasts,

speaker: PRINCE
text: That quench the fire of your pernicious rage

speaker: PRINCE
text: With purple fountains issuing from your veins:

speaker: PRINCE
text: On pain of torture, from those bloody hands

speaker: PRINCE
text: Throw your mistempered weapons to the ground,

speaker: PRINCE
text: And hear the sentence of your movèd prince.

speaker: PRINCE
text: Three civil brawls bred of an airy word

speaker: PRINCE
text: By thee, old Capulet, and Montague,

speaker: PRINCE
text: Have thrice disturbed the quiet of our streets

speaker: PRINCE
text: And made Verona’s ancient citizens

speaker: PRINCE
text: Cast by their grave-beseeming ornaments

speaker: PRINCE
text: To wield old partisans in hands as old,

speaker: PRINCE
text: Cankered with peace, to part your cankered hate.

speaker: PRINCE
text: If ever you disturb our streets again,

speaker: PRINCE
text: Your lives shall pay the forfeit of the peace.

speaker: PRINCE
text: For this time all the rest depart away.

speaker: PRINCE
text: You, Capulet, shall go along with me,

speaker: PRINCE
text: And, Montague, come you this afternoon

speaker: PRINCE
text: To know our farther pleasure in this case,

speaker: PRINCE
text: To old Free-town, our common judgment-place.

speaker: PRINCE
text: Once more, on pain of death, all men depart.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Who set this ancient quarrel new abroach?

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Speak, nephew, were you by when it began?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Here were the servants of your adversary,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: And yours, close fighting ere I did approach.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: I drew to part them. In the instant came

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: The fiery Tybalt with his sword prepared,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Which, as he breathed defiance to my ears,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: He swung about his head and cut the winds,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Who, nothing hurt withal, hissed him in scorn.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: While we were interchanging thrusts and blows

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Came more and more and fought on part and part,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Till the Prince came, who parted either part.

speaker:
LADY
               
              MONTAGUE
text: O, where is Romeo? Saw you him today?

speaker:
LADY
               
              MONTAGUE
text: Right glad I am he was not at this fray.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Madam, an hour before the worshiped sun

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Peered forth the golden window of the east,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: A troubled mind drove me to walk abroad,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Where underneath the grove of sycamore

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: That westward rooteth from this city side,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: So early walking did I see your son.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Towards him I made, but he was ’ware of me

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: And stole into the covert of the wood.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: I, measuring his affections by my own

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: (Which then most sought where most might not be found,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Being one too many by my weary self),

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Pursued my humor, not pursuing his,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: And gladly shunned who gladly fled from me.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Many a morning hath he there been seen,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: With tears augmenting the fresh morning’s dew,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Adding to clouds more clouds with his deep sighs.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: But all so soon as the all-cheering sun

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Should in the farthest east begin to draw

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: The shady curtains from Aurora’s bed,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Away from light steals home my heavy son

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: And private in his chamber pens himself,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Shuts up his windows, locks fair daylight out,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: And makes himself an artificial night.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Black and portentous must this humor prove,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Unless good counsel may the cause remove.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: My noble uncle, do you know the cause?

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: I neither know it nor can learn of him.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Have you importuned him by any means?

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Both by myself and many other friends.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: But he, his own affections’ counselor,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Is to himself—I will not say how true,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: But to himself so secret and so close,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: So far from sounding and discovery,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: As is the bud bit with an envious worm

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Ere he can spread his sweet leaves to the air

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Or dedicate his beauty to the same.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: Could we but learn from whence his sorrows grow,

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: We would as willingly give cure as know.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: See where he comes. So please you, step aside.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: I’ll know his grievance or be much denied.

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: I would thou wert so happy by thy stay

speaker: MONTAGUE
text: To hear true shrift.—Come, madam, let’s away.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Good morrow, cousin.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Is the day so young?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: But new struck nine.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Ay me, sad hours seem long.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Was that my father that went hence so fast?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: It was. What sadness lengthens Romeo’s hours?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Not having that which, having, makes them short.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: In love?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Out—

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Of love?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Out of her favor where I am in love.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Alas that love, so gentle in his view,

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Should be so tyrannous and rough in proof!

speaker: ROMEO
text: Alas that love, whose view is muffled still,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Should without eyes see pathways to his will!

speaker: ROMEO
text: Where shall we dine?—O me! What fray was here?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Yet tell me not, for I have heard it all.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Here’s much to do with hate, but more with love.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Why then, O brawling love, O loving hate,

speaker: ROMEO
text: O anything of nothing first create!

speaker: ROMEO
text: O heavy lightness, serious vanity,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Misshapen chaos of well-seeming forms,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Feather of lead, bright smoke, cold fire, sick health,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Still-waking sleep that is not what it is!

speaker: ROMEO
text: This love feel I, that feel no love in this.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Dost thou not laugh?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: No, coz, I rather weep.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Good heart, at what?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: At thy good heart’s oppression.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Why, such is love’s transgression.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Griefs of mine own lie heavy in my breast,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Which thou wilt propagate to have it pressed

speaker: ROMEO
text: With more of thine. This love that thou hast shown

speaker: ROMEO
text: Doth add more grief to too much of mine own.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Love is a smoke made with the fume of sighs;

speaker: ROMEO
text: Being purged, a fire sparkling in lovers’ eyes;

speaker: ROMEO
text: Being vexed, a sea nourished with loving tears.

speaker: ROMEO
text: What is it else? A madness most discreet,

speaker: ROMEO
text: A choking gall, and a preserving sweet.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Farewell, my coz.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Soft, I will go along.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: An if you leave me so, you do me wrong.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Tut, I have lost myself. I am not here.

speaker: ROMEO
text: This is not Romeo. He’s some other where.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Tell me in sadness, who is that you love?

speaker: ROMEO
text: What, shall I groan and tell thee?

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Groan? Why, no. But sadly tell me who.

speaker: ROMEO
text: A sick man in sadness makes his will—

speaker: ROMEO
text: A word ill urged to one that is so ill.

speaker: ROMEO
text: In sadness, cousin, I do love a woman.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: I aimed so near when I supposed you loved.

speaker: ROMEO
text: A right good markman! And she’s fair I love.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: A right fair mark, fair coz, is soonest hit.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Well in that hit you miss. She’ll not be hit

speaker: ROMEO
text: With Cupid’s arrow. She hath Dian’s wit,

speaker: ROMEO
text: And, in strong proof of chastity well armed,

speaker: ROMEO
text: From love’s weak childish bow she lives uncharmed.

speaker: ROMEO
text: She will not stay the siege of loving terms,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Nor bide th’ encounter of assailing eyes,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Nor ope her lap to saint-seducing gold.

speaker: ROMEO
text: O, she is rich in beauty, only poor

speaker: ROMEO
text: That, when she dies, with beauty dies her store.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Then she hath sworn that she will still live chaste?

speaker: ROMEO
text: She hath, and in that sparing makes huge waste;

speaker: ROMEO
text: For beauty, starved with her severity,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Cuts beauty off from all posterity.

speaker: ROMEO
text: She is too fair, too wise, wisely too fair,

speaker: ROMEO
text: To merit bliss by making me despair.

speaker: ROMEO
text: She hath forsworn to love, and in that vow

speaker: ROMEO
text: Do I live dead, that live to tell it now.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Be ruled by me. Forget to think of her.

speaker: ROMEO
text: O, teach me how I should forget to think!

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: By giving liberty unto thine eyes.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: Examine other beauties.

speaker: ROMEO
text: ’Tis the way

speaker: ROMEO
text: To call hers, exquisite, in question more.

speaker: ROMEO
text: These happy masks that kiss fair ladies’ brows,

speaker: ROMEO
text: Being black, puts us in mind they hide the fair.

speaker: ROMEO
text: He that is strucken blind cannot forget

speaker: ROMEO
text: The precious treasure of his eyesight lost.

speaker: ROMEO
text: Show me a mistress that is passing fair;

speaker: ROMEO
text: What doth her beauty serve but as a note

speaker: ROMEO
text: Where I may read who passed that passing fair?

speaker: ROMEO
text: Farewell. Thou canst not teach me to forget.

speaker: BENVOLIO
text: I’ll pay that doctrine or else die in debt.
